blocks.text: What is Cambridge A Level Chemistry Paper 5?

Cambridge International A Level Chemistry (9701) Paper 5 is the Planning, Analysis and Evaluation paper. It is a written paper — not a laboratory paper — that tests the higher-order experimental skills of planning experimental investigations, analysing data, drawing conclusions and evaluating experimental procedures. Paper 5 is worth 30 marks (11.5% of the A Level grade) and must be completed in 1 hour 15 minutes.

Paper 5 differs fundamentally from Paper 3 (the laboratory practical). No laboratory equipment is required for Paper 5 — all questions are answered in writing. However, the syllabus is clear that candidates cannot be adequately prepared for Paper 5 without extensive laboratory experience at A Level standard. The skills tested in Paper 5 are directly derived from, and reinforced by, practical work conducted throughout the course.

The context of Paper 5 questions may be outside the syllabus content — you will not be penalised for lacking knowledge of a specific chemistry outside the syllabus, because additional information is always provided. What is being tested is your ability to apply experimental reasoning, data analysis and evaluation to any chemical context.

🔑 KEY FACTS ABOUT PAPER 5
Paper 5 is a written paper — no laboratory work is required in the exam itself. All answers are produced in writing.

The context of questions may be outside the syllabus. You will always receive all the information about the specific chemistry that you need. What is being tested is your experimental reasoning — not specialist knowledge of the context.
Paper 5 has 30 marks in 75 minutes — 2.5 minutes per mark. A 6-mark planning question gets ~15 minutes. Time allocation is tighter than Paper 4, so efficiency is critical.
The paper consists of two or more questions totalling 30 marks. Questions typically combine a planning task (defining variables, describing the method) with a data analysis and evaluation task.
Marks are split approximately 40% planning and 60% analysis/evaluation, though this varies by paper. Both halves must be prepared thoroughly.

Cambridge consistently states in examiner reports that inadequate practical experience is the single biggest cause of underperformance on Paper 5. Candidates who do not engage with laboratory work during the course are severely disadvantaged.

blocks.text: The Anatomy of a Paper 5 Question

rows: ✅ EXAMINER TIP

A Paper 5 question typically begins with a short paragraph describing an experimental investigation. Read this TWICE before answering anything. Identify: (1) What is being investigated? (2) What is the independent variable? (3) What is the dependent variable? (4) What needs to be controlled?

Planning sub-parts ('describe a method', 'identify the variables', 'suggest how to process the data') require systematic, specific answers. Vague answers ('heat the mixture', 'measure the volume') consistently lose marks. Always state what, how, and why.

Data analysis sub-parts always provide data (a table, a graph, or both). Read the data carefully before attempting any calculation. Identify the units; check the scale; note any anomalous points before calculating.

Evaluation sub-parts reward structured, specific critique. Do not write 'it is difficult to measure accurately'. State WHICH measurement, WHY it is difficult, HOW significant the error is, and HOW to improve it.

sections.title: SECTION 2: COMMAND WORDS & MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE

blocks.text: Paper 5 Command Words — Planning & Evaluation Focus

All Cambridge command words from Papers 2 and 4 appear in Paper 5. However, Paper 5 uses a specific set of command words in the context of experimental design and evaluation. These have very precise meanings that are different from their use in theory papers.

rows: Command Word
Paper 5 Specific Usage
Mark Scheme Expectation
Common Error
PLAN / DESCRIBE A METHOD
Describe the complete experimental procedure for an investigation from start to finish
Must include: apparatus, quantities/concentrations, procedure steps, data collection strategy, data processing method. Each missing component loses marks.
Writing only a list of apparatus without describing the procedure steps; omitting how the independent variable is varied or the dependent variable is measured
IDENTIFY (variables)
State the independent, dependent and key control variables for the experiment
Independent variable: what you deliberately change. Dependent variable: what you measure as a result. Control variables: what you keep constant and HOW.
Confusing independent and dependent variables; listing control variables without stating HOW to keep them constant
PREDICT (or SKETCH a graph)
State or draw the expected relationship between variables, with a justification
State the direction (linear/curve/proportional/inverse); indicate gradient sign; give chemical reason for the predicted relationship.
Drawing a graph without labelling axes with units; predicting a trend without a chemical justification
EVALUATE
Critically assess the experimental procedure or data provided
Must identify specific weaknesses; explain WHY each is a problem; suggest realistic, specific improvements. Both weakness AND improvement must be paired.
Writing 'human error' as a source of error (never acceptable); failing to pair each weakness with an improvement; identifying trivial errors rather than significant ones
SUGGEST AN IMPROVEMENT
Propose a realistic change to the experimental method that would reduce a specific error
Must be specific to the identified error, chemically/physically realistic, and explain why it improves the result.
Suggesting 'be more careful' or 'repeat the experiment' without specifying what structural change to the method would reduce the error
CALCULATE (from graph data)
Use a plotted graph to determine a value (gradient, intercept, area)
Show clearly: which two points were used for gradient; give the co-ordinates; show the calculation. The points must be more than half the drawn line apart.
Using data points that are too close together (leading to large percentage error in gradient); not showing the working
COMMENT ON (data quality)
Assess whether the data provided is reliable and what it does or does not support
Must address: scatter of points around line of best fit; presence of anomalous points; range of data collected; whether the data support the prediction.
Commenting only on anomalous points without addressing the overall scatter or the range of data

blocks.text: High-Value Phrases for Paper 5 Answers

rows: 🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — PLANNING

INDEPENDENT VARIABLE: 'The independent variable is [X]. It will be varied by [specific method, e.g., preparing a series of solutions of different concentrations using serial dilution / by changing the temperature using a water bath set at different temperatures].'

DEPENDENT VARIABLE: 'The dependent variable is [Y]. It will be measured by [specific method, e.g., recording the absorbance using a colorimeter / timing the reaction until the cross is no longer visible / using a pH meter calibrated before use].'

CONTROL VARIABLES: 'The [variable, e.g., temperature / concentration of X / total volume of solution] must be controlled. This will be achieved by [specific method, e.g., using a thermostatted water bath / using the same concentration throughout / measuring all volumes with a pipette].'

SAFETY PRECAUTION: 'A fume hood should be used because [named substance] produces toxic/corrosive fumes. Chemically resistant gloves should be worn when handling [corrosive substance]. Eye protection must be worn at all times.'

DATA PROCESSING: 'Plot a graph of [dependent variable with units] on the y-axis against [independent variable with units] on the x-axis. A straight-line graph through the origin would confirm [the relationship]. The gradient of the line gives [the value of the constant k / the rate constant].'
🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — ANALYSIS & EVALUATION

GRADIENT CALCULATION: 'Using the two points (x₁, y₁) and (x₂, y₂) which are more than half the length of the line apart: gradient = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁) = [value] [units]. The units of the gradient are [Y units / X units].'

ANOMALOUS POINT: 'The data point at [specific value, e.g., t = 60 s, concentration = 0.25 mol dm⁻³] is anomalous — it lies significantly off the line of best fit. This may have been caused by [specific reason: incomplete mixing before timing began / contamination of the sample]. This point should be repeated and then excluded from the line of best fit if confirmed as anomalous.'

ERROR ANALYSIS — SYSTEMATIC: 'The temperature of the reaction mixture may not be equal to the temperature of the water bath, because heat transfer through the glass takes time. This would cause a systematic error: the actual temperature of the reaction is consistently lower than the recorded temperature. This error could be minimised by [allowing more time for thermal equilibration / using a thermometer placed directly in the reaction mixture].'

ERROR ANALYSIS — RANDOM: 'The time measurement is subject to random error because the colour change is gradual and the exact end-point is difficult to judge consistently. This introduces random errors with no fixed sign. This error could be reduced by [using a colorimeter to monitor absorbance continuously / using a more sharply defined colour change indicator].'

PERCENTAGE ERROR STATEMENT: 'The uncertainty in the burette reading is ±0.05 cm³ per reading; for a titre of 12.35 cm³ (two readings), the maximum percentage error = (2 × 0.05 / 12.35) × 100 = 0.81%. This is [acceptable / significant] relative to the overall precision required.'

RELIABILITY COMMENT: 'The data are reliable because the points show little scatter about the line of best fit and there are no anomalous values. The range of [independent variable] is sufficient ([X min] to [X max]) to establish the trend clearly. Three repeats at each concentration were used to calculate a mean, reducing the effect of random errors.'

CONCLUSION STATEMENT: 'The data support the hypothesis that [stated relationship, e.g., the rate of reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of H₂O₂]. The graph shows a [linear relationship / directly proportional relationship (passing through the origin)] between [Y] and [X]. The gradient of the line gives a value of [k = gradient value with units].'

blocks.text: Overview: 8 Years of Cambridge Chemistry Paper 5

The following analysis covers all Cambridge A Level Chemistry Paper 5 variants (variants 51, 52, 53) from 2018 to 2025. Each variant is 30 marks. Across 8 years with multiple variants, this represents analysis of over 720 individual marks — providing a precise picture of which skills are tested most consistently and which experimental contexts Cambridge uses most frequently.

rows: 🔑 KEY FACTS FROM 8-YEAR ANALYSIS
Planning questions (defining the problem and method) account for approximately 12–15 marks per paper — the single largest mark allocation of any skill category.
Data analysis — specifically graph plotting, gradient calculation, and conclusion drawing — accounts for approximately 10–12 marks per paper across all years analysed.

Evaluation questions (error analysis and suggesting improvements) appear in every Paper 5 and account for approximately 5–8 marks per paper. These marks are consistently the hardest to score at full marks.
Rate of reaction experiments are the single most frequently recurring experimental context in Paper 5, appearing in some form in approximately 70% of papers analysed (2018–2025).

Colorimetry, titration, thermometric experiments, and gas volume measurements are all regularly used as experimental contexts. The specific chemistry varies — the underlying experimental reasoning remains consistent.

AO3 marks for 'evaluation' are the most often lost at A* level — candidates who score well on planning and analysis frequently drop marks for incomplete or vague evaluation. Specific, paired (weakness + improvement) answers are required.

📊 HOW PAPER 5 MARKS ARE DISTRIBUTED
Planning (defining variables + method): ~40–42% of total marks (12–13 marks). The most mark-dense single area. Every planning sub-part must be answered in specific detail.

Data analysis and conclusion (graph + calculations + interpretation): ~40–43% of marks (12–13 marks). Graph plotting alone accounts for 4–5 marks — marks that are available to every student who has practised the skill.

Evaluation (error analysis + improvements + data quality): ~18–20% of marks (5–6 marks). The hardest marks to earn at full credit — examiners consistently note that students give vague or non-specific answers.

The 30 marks of Paper 5 are available in a shorter time than any other paper (2.5 min/mark). Efficient, structured answers are essential. Avoid over-elaborating on low-mark sub-parts at the expense of high-mark planning and graph questions.

blocks.text: Why Paper 5 Is Unique in the Chemistry Assessment

Paper 5 is the only Cambridge A Level Chemistry paper that tests AO3 skills exclusively and in a written (non-laboratory) context. This makes it unique: you are being asked to demonstrate laboratory expertise without actually performing any practical work. Every mark on Paper 5 is earned by applying systematic experimental reasoning — the ability to THINK like a scientist, not just recall science.

rows: 🔑 KEY FACTS
Paper 5 is the only paper in the A Level where AO1 (knowledge recall) scores zero marks. Every single mark requires demonstrating AO3: experimental planning, data analysis or evaluation.

The most reliable way to fail Paper 5 is to write vague answers. Every sentence must contain a specific chemical or physical detail. 'Heat carefully' earns no marks. 'Heat using a water bath thermostatted at [specified temperature]' earns marks.
Paper 5 data is always provided in the question. You never need to generate data. The exam tests whether you can extract meaning from data — not whether you can perform an experiment.

In Paper 5, 'suggest an improvement' requires a specific structural change to the experimental method — not encouragement to be more precise or careful. Cambridge never awards marks for 'repeat the experiment' as a standalone improvement.

blocks.text: Strategy 1: Planning Questions — Defining the Problem

rows: ✍️ ANSWERING STRATEGY

IDENTIFY VARIABLES SYSTEMATICALLY: Always identify all three types: (1) INDEPENDENT variable — what you deliberately change and its range; (2) DEPENDENT variable — what you measure as a result and how you measure it; (3) KEY CONTROL variables — what you keep constant AND how you achieve this control. Cambridge awards separate marks for naming the variable AND stating how it is controlled.

WRITE A PREDICTION AS A HYPOTHESIS: A prediction must take the form: 'As [independent variable] increases, [dependent variable] will [increase/decrease/remain constant] because [chemical reason].' If asked for a graph sketch, label both axes with the variable name AND units; show the predicted shape (line, curve, directly proportional) and whether it passes through the origin.

IDENTIFY RISKS SPECIFICALLY: Risk assessments must name the specific hazardous substance, the specific hazard (corrosive, toxic, flammable), and the specific precaution (fume hood, resistant gloves, face shield, no naked flames). Generic statements ('wear safety goggles') are worth no marks in isolation.

STATE CONCENTRATIONS AND VOLUMES: Where a planning question asks for a method, always state specific quantities: volumes (in cm³), concentrations (in mol dm⁻³) and masses (in g). Approximate values are accepted, but completely omitting quantities loses planning marks consistently.

blocks.text: Strategy 2: Planning Questions — The Method

rows: ✍️ ANSWERING STRATEGY

USE A STEP-BY-STEP FORMAT: Write the method as a numbered procedure. Each step should describe one clear action. This prevents missing steps and makes it easy for examiners to award marks to each component.

DESCRIBE HOW TO VARY THE INDEPENDENT VARIABLE: Never simply state that the independent variable 'will be changed'. Describe the specific mechanism: 'Prepare six solutions of HCl with concentrations 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50 and 0.60 mol dm⁻³ by diluting a stock solution of known concentration.' This level of specificity is required for full planning marks.

DESCRIBE HOW DATA WILL BE PROCESSED: Every planning answer should end by stating: (1) what graph to plot (y-axis variable vs x-axis variable, with units); (2) what a specific graph shape would indicate; (3) how to extract the key value from the graph (e.g., 'the gradient of the linear graph = k, the rate constant'). Cambridge consistently awards marks for this data processing description.

INCLUDE CONCORDANCY FOR TITRATIONS: If the method involves titration, always state: 'Repeat the titration until two concordant values are obtained (within 0.10 cm³ of each other). Calculate the mean of the concordant values and use this in subsequent calculations.'

DESCRIBE HOW TO CONTROL KEY VARIABLES: For each control variable identified, state the specific instrument or technique: 'Temperature is controlled by immersing the reaction flask in a water bath thermostatted to [specified temperature] ± 0.5°C, with a thermometer placed directly in the reaction mixture to verify the temperature.'

blocks.text: Strategy 3: Data Analysis — Plotting and Interpreting Graphs

rows: ✍️ ANSWERING STRATEGY

GRAPH PLOTTING — MANDATORY ELEMENTS (4–5 marks typically): (1) Both axes labelled with the correct variable name AND units [1]; (2) Scale chosen so data points occupy at least half the grid in both directions, using a sensible scale (1, 2, 5 units per division) [1]; (3) All data points plotted correctly to within half a small square [1 per point, up to total]; (4) Line of best fit drawn — either a straight line or a smooth curve, with points distributed approximately evenly on both sides [1]; (5) Anomalous points identified (circled or noted) and NOT included in the line of best fit [1].

GRADIENT CALCULATION: Always use two points that are MORE than half the drawn line apart (NOT tabulated data points unless they happen to lie exactly on the line). Write: 'Using points (x₁, y₁) = (value, value) and (x₂, y₂) = (value, value): gradient = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁) = [value] [units].' Show units explicitly — gradient units = y-axis units / x-axis units.

INTERCEPT READING: Read the y-intercept from the graph at x = 0. If the graph does not extend to x = 0, extrapolate the line and read the intercept from the extrapolation. State: 'Extrapolating the line to x = 0 gives a y-intercept of [value] [units].'

INTERPRETING GRAPH SHAPE: Describe what the graph shows specifically: 'The graph shows a linear (straight-line) relationship, passing through the origin, indicating that [dependent variable] is directly proportional to [independent variable].' Or: 'The graph shows a curve with decreasing gradient, consistent with [first-order / exponential decay / equilibrium approached].' Always link the shape to a chemical interpretation.

blocks.text: Strategy 4: Conclusion and Scientific Explanation

rows: ✍️ ANSWERING STRATEGY

THREE-PART CONCLUSION STRUCTURE: (1) STATE the relationship shown by the data/graph (what shape, what direction); (2) COMPARE to the prediction or hypothesis (does the data support or refute it?); (3) EXPLAIN using chemistry (what principle explains the observed relationship?).

USE NUMERICAL EVIDENCE: A conclusion that says 'the rate increases as concentration increases' earns fewer marks than 'the rate doubled when [A] doubled (from 0.10 to 0.20 mol dm⁻³, rate increased from 2.4×10⁻³ to 4.8×10⁻³ mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹), consistent with first-order kinetics'. Cite the actual data values.

LINK TO THEORY: After describing what the data shows, always explain WHY it makes chemical sense: 'This is consistent with the collision theory — at higher concentrations, there are more reactant particles per unit volume, increasing the frequency of effective collisions and therefore the rate of reaction.'

ADDRESS THE HYPOTHESIS EXPLICITLY: If a hypothesis was stated in an earlier sub-part, explicitly state whether the results support or refute it, and with what confidence: 'These results support the hypothesis that the reaction is first-order with respect to [A]. All data points lie close to the line of best fit with no systematic deviation, suggesting the relationship is reliable.'

blocks.text: Strategy 5: Evaluation — Error Analysis and Improvement

rows: ✍️ ANSWERING STRATEGY

ALWAYS PAIR WEAKNESS WITH IMPROVEMENT: Cambridge consistently states in examiner reports that evaluations must be paired. Format: 'WEAKNESS: [specific error source and why it matters]. IMPROVEMENT: [specific structural change to the method that addresses this weakness].' An unpaired weakness or an unpaired improvement earns zero or half marks.

DISTINGUISH SYSTEMATIC FROM RANDOM ERRORS: SYSTEMATIC ERROR: consistent bias in one direction (e.g., zero error on balance, heat loss from calorimeter, light absorption by the container). RANDOM ERROR: inconsistent variation in both directions (e.g., timing a colour change, reading a meniscus). Identify which type for each error source.

CALCULATE PERCENTAGE ERRORS: For the most significant measurement, always calculate: Percentage error = (2 × instrument uncertainty / measured value) × 100. For a burette (±0.05 cm³) and a titre of 12.35 cm³: % error = (2 × 0.05 / 12.35) × 100 = 0.81%. Compare this to the concentration uncertainty in the final result.

NEVER WRITE 'HUMAN ERROR': This phrase is not acceptable to Cambridge examiners. Replace with the specific action subject to error: 'The timing of the reaction is subject to random error because the colour change endpoint is gradual and difficult to judge consistently.' This earns marks; 'human error in timing' does not.

SUGGEST REALISTIC IMPROVEMENTS: Improvements must be physically possible and genuinely reduce the identified error. 'Use a more accurate thermometer' is acceptable only if you specify the precision required. 'Use a thermocouple thermometer with ±0.1°C precision instead of a mercury thermometer (±0.5°C)' is a full-mark improvement.

blocks.text: Strategy 6: Extended Planning Responses

rows: ✍️ ANSWERING STRATEGY

PLAN BEFORE WRITING: For any planning sub-part worth 5+ marks, spend 60 seconds writing a bullet plan before starting your answer. Identify: variables (3 types), apparatus, procedure steps, data processing, graph to plot. Ensure your plan covers all these before writing prose.

USE THE SYLLABUS SKILLS LIST AS A CHECKLIST: The Paper 5 Planning skills include: identify variables; identify apparatus; suggest concentrations; describe procedure; describe safety; describe how to vary the independent variable; describe data processing; describe what graph to plot and how to interpret it. Scan this list for every planning answer.
WRITE NUMBERED STEPS FOR PROCEDURES: Extended method descriptions should use numbered steps. This makes it impossible to accidentally skip a step and makes the structure clear to the examiner.

Data analysis sub-parts (graph)

✅ EXAMINER TIP
2.5 minutes per mark in Paper 5 is the tightest time ratio in the A Level. A 6-mark planning question gets 15 minutes maximum. Do not over-write on early low-mark sub-parts.

Graph plotting marks (4–5 marks) are the most reliable marks on Paper 5 — they are available to every candidate who practises the skill. Never rush the graph to answer written questions first; the graph marks are highly predictable.

blocks.text: Essential Knowledge & Key Marking Points

A complete method must include: (1) how to set up the apparatus; (2) how to vary the independent variable across an appropriate range; (3) how to measure the dependent variable with appropriate precision; (4) how to control key variables; (5) a data recording strategy; (6) a data processing strategy (what to plot, what to calculate).

Apparatus must be named specifically. 'A measuring device' earns no marks. '25 cm³ pipette (±0.06 cm³)' or 'burette (±0.05 cm³)' earns marks. Precision of the apparatus must be appropriate to the measurement required.

Quantities: always state approximate volumes, concentrations and masses. For a titration: '25.0 cm³ of [substance] using a pipette; approximately 0.10 mol dm⁻³ [reagent] in the burette.' Cambridge accepts approximate values — complete omission of quantities loses marks.
Safety: name the hazardous substance; identify the hazard (toxic, corrosive, oxidising); state the precaution (fume hood, gloves, face shield, no naked flames). Each element is separately marked.

Data processing: always state what to plot (y-axis vs x-axis with units); what line shape to expect; what the gradient or intercept represents. This is the most commonly omitted component of planning answers.

rows: 🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — PLANNING METHOD

APPARATUS: 'A burette (±0.05 cm³) will be used to deliver [reagent]. A 25 cm³ pipette (±0.06 cm³) will be used to measure [substance]. A thermostatted water bath (±0.5°C) will maintain temperature. A colorimeter with appropriate filter will measure absorbance of the solution.'

VARYING THE INDEPENDENT VARIABLE: 'A series of six solutions will be prepared with [independent variable] values of [min, values, max]. This will be achieved by [specific method: dilution from a stock solution / adding fixed increments of solid / adjusting pH using a pH meter with standard buffer solutions].'

CONTROLLING TEMPERATURE: 'All reactions will be carried out in a water bath thermostatted at [temperature] ± 0.5°C. A thermometer placed in the reaction vessel will confirm the temperature before timing begins. At least 3 minutes equilibration time will be allowed before adding the second reagent.'

RATE EXPERIMENTS: 'The time from mixing the reagents to [observable endpoint: first appearance of precipitate / cross becoming invisible / solution becoming colourless] will be recorded using a stopwatch. The rate will be calculated as 1/t. A graph of 1/t against [independent variable] will be plotted.'

TITRATION CONCORDANCY: 'The titration will be repeated until two concordant readings within 0.10 cm³ of each other are obtained. The mean of these concordant readings will be used in all calculations. A rough titration will be performed first to identify the approximate endpoint.'
❌ AVOID THIS ERROR

VAGUE METHOD STEPS: Writing 'mix the solutions and observe' loses all method marks for that step. Every step must state WHAT is mixed, in WHAT quantity, using WHAT apparatus, and WHAT the observation is.

MISSING DATA PROCESSING: Stopping the method description after describing how to collect data — without stating what graph to plot or what calculation to perform — loses 2–3 marks that are very easily available.

INADEQUATE CONTROL VARIABLES: Stating 'temperature must be kept constant' without describing HOW it will be kept constant (water bath, thermometer, equilibration time) earns zero marks for that control variable.

GENERIC SAFETY STATEMENTS: Writing 'wear eye protection' as the only safety comment earns zero marks. The specific hazard and specific precaution must be linked to the specific substances in the experiment.
SKILL: PLANNING — DEFINING THE PROBLEM ★★★★★ CRITICAL │ Avg ~5 marks per paper

blocks.text: Essential Knowledge & Key Marking Points
Independent variable: the variable that is DELIBERATELY CHANGED by the experimenter. State the name AND the range of values to be tested AND how it will be changed.
Dependent variable: the variable that is MEASURED as a result of changing the independent variable. State the name AND the instrument used to measure it AND its precision.
Control variables: all other variables that could affect the dependent variable. State the name AND the method of control AND why controlling it matters for validity.

Prediction/hypothesis: a statement of the expected relationship between independent and dependent variables, with a chemical reason. If a graph sketch is requested: label both axes (variable name + units); show the expected shape; indicate whether the graph passes through the origin.
The aim of the experiment: state this in terms of the relationship being investigated — 'to investigate the effect of [independent variable] on [dependent variable] for [specified system].'

rows: 🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — VARIABLES

INDEPENDENT VARIABLE: 'The independent variable is the concentration of hydrogen peroxide, [H₂O₂]. This will be varied from 0.10 to 0.50 mol dm⁻³ in increments of 0.10 mol dm⁻³ by diluting a stock solution of known concentration with distilled water.'

DEPENDENT VARIABLE: 'The dependent variable is the initial rate of reaction. This will be determined by measuring the time, t, from mixing the reagents to the disappearance of the blue-black colour of the starch-iodine complex. The rate will be taken as proportional to 1/t.'

CONTROL VARIABLE: 'Temperature must be controlled because the rate constant k is temperature-dependent; any variation in temperature would change the rate independently of the concentration change. Temperature will be controlled by thermostatting the reaction vessel in a water bath set at 25°C ± 0.5°C.'

PREDICTION: 'I predict that as the concentration of [A] increases, the initial rate of reaction will increase. Based on the collision theory, a higher concentration provides more reactant particles per unit volume, increasing the frequency of effective collisions per unit time.'
SKILL: DATA ANALYSIS — GRAPH PLOTTING ★★★★★ CRITICAL │ Avg ~5 marks per paper

blocks.text: Essential Knowledge & Key Marking Points

Axes must be labelled with the variable name AND units in the correct format: 'Rate of reaction / mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹' or 'Rate of reaction (mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹)'. Both conventions are acceptable. Missing units costs a mark.

Scale: the plotted points must occupy at least half of the grid in both the x and y directions. Use a scale that allows easy reading: 1, 2 or 5 units per grid square. NEVER use 3, 6, 7 or 9 units per square — these are unacceptable.
Plotting accuracy: each point must be plotted within half a small square of its correct position. Use a small cross (×) or circled dot (⊙). Do not use blobs or large dots.

Line of best fit: draw either a straight line or a smooth curve. The line/curve should have approximately equal numbers of data points on each side along its entire length. Do not force the line through the origin unless the data support this.

Anomalous points: identify any point(s) that lie significantly off the line of best fit. Circle them clearly. Do NOT include anomalous points when drawing the line of best fit. State in writing: 'The point at ([x], [y]) is anomalous and has been excluded from the line of best fit.'

Gradient calculation: choose two points on the drawn line (not tabulated data points) that are more than half the length of the drawn line apart. Show the co-ordinates of both points. Calculate gradient = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁) and state units.

rows: 🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — GRAPHS
AXES LABELLING: 'ln (rate) / units' or '1/T / K⁻¹' — always show both the variable and its units on each axis.

SCALE CHOICE: If x-axis data ranges from 0.10 to 0.50 (range 0.40), choose a scale of 0.10 per division on a 4-division grid, or 0.05 per division on an 8-division grid. The plotted points should extend across at least 4–5 divisions in each direction.

GRADIENT: 'Using the two points (0.10, 2.4×10⁻³) and (0.50, 12.0×10⁻³) from the line of best fit: gradient = (12.0×10⁻³ − 2.4×10⁻³) / (0.50 − 0.10) = 9.6×10⁻³ / 0.40 = 2.4×10⁻² [units: mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹ / mol dm⁻³ = s⁻¹].'

ANOMALOUS POINT: 'The point at concentration = 0.30 mol dm⁻³ appears anomalous — it lies noticeably above the line of best fit compared to the general trend. It has been circled and excluded from the line of best fit. This data point should be repeated.'
❌ AVOID THIS ERROR

USING TABULATED DATA POINTS FOR GRADIENT: Choosing the first and last rows of the data table as the gradient points. These may not lie on the line of best fit. Always use points that you read OFF the drawn line.
WRONG SCALE: Using a scale of 3 units per division (e.g., 0, 3, 6, 9). This makes accurate plotting impossible and loses the scale mark. Always use 1, 2, or 5 per division.
JOINING POINTS WITH STRAIGHT LINES BETWEEN EACH: Drawing a 'dot-to-dot' line through each point rather than a single line or curve of best fit. This loses the line of best fit mark.
PLOTTING OUTSIDE THE GRID: Choosing a scale that leaves the last few data points off the edge of the grid. Always check the full range of data before choosing the scale.
SKILL: EVALUATION — ERROR ANALYSIS & IMPROVEMENTS ★★★★☆ HIGH │ Avg ~4 marks per paper

blocks.text: Essential Knowledge & Key Marking Points

Systematic errors: consistent bias in one direction. Examples: heat loss in calorimetry (consistently underestimates temperature change → underestimates ΔH); zero error on a balance; light absorption by the reaction vessel in colorimetry. Effect: shifts ALL results in the same direction.

Random errors: vary in magnitude and direction from measurement to measurement. Examples: timing a colour change endpoint; reading the meniscus in a burette; estimating the temperature from a non-calibrated thermometer. Effect: increases scatter of data; reduced by repeating and averaging.

Percentage error formula: % error = (instrument uncertainty / measured value) × 100. For a burette reading: uncertainty = 0.05 cm³. For a temperature change: uncertainty = 2 × 0.5°C = 1°C (two temperature readings, each ±0.5°C).

Paired weakness-improvement format is mandatory. Each evaluation point must: (1) name the specific source of error; (2) explain why it matters (what effect it has on the result); (3) suggest a specific, realistic improvement that reduces that specific error.

Significant improvements (worth proposing): use of a data logger/colorimeter instead of visual endpoint judgment; water bath instead of measuring temperature of the surroundings; use of a burette instead of a measuring cylinder; repeating the measurement and averaging; increasing the range of the independent variable.

rows: 🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — EVALUATION

SYSTEMATIC ERROR (HEAT LOSS): 'WEAKNESS: Heat loss from the calorimeter to the surroundings is a systematic error that causes the measured temperature change to be consistently lower than the actual temperature change. This leads to a systematic underestimate of the enthalpy change. IMPROVEMENT: Use a polystyrene cup inside a larger polystyrene cup with a lid, or use a vacuum-jacketed calorimeter, to minimise heat loss to the surroundings.'

RANDOM ERROR (TIMING): 'WEAKNESS: The endpoint of the clock reaction (disappearance of the blue-black starch-iodine colour) is gradual rather than instantaneous. This makes it difficult to define the exact moment of completion, introducing a random error in the time measurement. IMPROVEMENT: Use a colorimeter to monitor the absorbance of the solution continuously, defining the endpoint as when the absorbance reaches a specific pre-determined value (e.g., zero).'

PERCENTAGE ERROR: 'The temperature change measured is approximately 5.0°C. The uncertainty in each thermometer reading is ±0.5°C; for two readings, the maximum uncertainty is ±1.0°C. Percentage error = (1.0/5.0) × 100 = 20%. This is the dominant source of error in the calculation. Using a thermistor or thermocouple with ±0.1°C precision would reduce this to 4%.'

IMPROVEMENT — INCREASING RANGE: 'Only three concentrations were tested in the range 0.10–0.30 mol dm⁻³. This limited range makes it difficult to establish whether the relationship is linear across a wider range of concentrations. Testing at least 6 concentrations across a wider range (0.05–0.60 mol dm⁻³) would provide more reliable evidence for the trend.'
SKILL: CONCLUSION — INTERPRETATION & SCIENTIFIC EXPLANATION ★★★★☆ HIGH │ Avg ~3 marks per paper

blocks.text: Essential Knowledge & Key Marking Points
State what the data or graph shows: describe the relationship (linear, directly proportional, inversely proportional, exponential, no trend). Cite numerical evidence from the data or graph.
Compare to the prediction or hypothesis: explicitly state whether the results support or refute the prediction. If partially supported, state which part is supported and which is not.

Provide a scientific explanation: link the observed relationship to the relevant chemical principle (collision theory, equilibrium, thermodynamics, etc.). This is an AO3 mark — the chemistry must be correct.
Address anomalous data explicitly: if anomalous points are present, acknowledge them and suggest why they might have occurred. Do not simply ignore them.
State confidence: based on the quality of the data (scatter, range, number of data points), state how confident you are in the conclusion. If data quality is poor, note this as a limitation.

rows: 🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — CONCLUSION

RELATIONSHIP DESCRIPTION: 'The graph shows a linear relationship between 1/t (proportional to rate) and [H₂O₂] concentration. The line of best fit passes through the origin (or close to it), consistent with a directly proportional relationship. This indicates that the rate of reaction is first-order with respect to [H₂O₂].'

COMPARISON TO PREDICTION: 'These results support the prediction that rate is directly proportional to [H₂O₂] concentration. The gradient of the graph gives k = 2.4×10⁻² s⁻¹, consistent with a first-order rate constant at 25°C. The data show minimal scatter about the line of best fit, indicating reliable measurements.'

SCIENTIFIC EXPLANATION: 'This result is consistent with the collision theory: doubling the concentration of [H₂O₂] doubles the number of [H₂O₂] molecules per unit volume, doubling the frequency of effective collisions with the iodide ions and therefore doubling the rate of reaction.'

ADDRESSING ANOMALY IN CONCLUSION: 'The data point at [concentration = 0.30 mol dm⁻³] is anomalous and lies significantly above the line of best fit. This may indicate a contamination in that particular sample or an error in preparation of that solution. Excluding this point, the remaining data strongly support a linear relationship.'
SKILL: EVALUATION — DATA QUALITY & RELIABILITY ★★★☆☆ MODERATE │ Avg ~1.5 marks per paper

blocks.text: Essential Knowledge & Key Marking Points

Scatter: assess how closely the data points lie to the line of best fit. Small scatter = precise measurements; large scatter = significant random error. State specifically which data points deviate most significantly.

Range: comment on whether the range of the independent variable is sufficient to establish the trend. If only 3 data points are available, note that a wider range and more data points would improve reliability.

Replication: state how many replicates were performed and whether this is sufficient. Single measurements without replication are unreliable; three repeats are standard; more repeats reduce the effect of random errors.

Whether data support the prediction: explicitly state whether the overall dataset supports the hypothesis, with reference to the quality of the fit to the line and whether the relationship (e.g., linearity through origin) is clearly established.

rows: 🏆 MARK SCHEME LANGUAGE — DATA QUALITY

SCATTER COMMENT: 'The data show relatively low scatter about the line of best fit — all points lie within [distance] of the line, suggesting the measurements are reproducible and the random errors are small relative to the size of the effect being measured.'

RANGE COMMENT: 'The range of [independent variable] tested (0.10 to 0.50 mol dm⁻³) is adequate to show the linear trend. However, testing higher concentrations (up to 1.0 mol dm⁻³) would confirm whether the linear relationship is maintained across a wider range, and would improve the precision of the gradient determination.'

REPLICATION: 'Only one reading was taken at each concentration. Repeating each measurement three times and calculating a mean value would reduce the effect of random errors and improve the reliability of the data. Anomalous readings could then be identified by comparison with replicate values.'

blocks.text: How to Use These Examples

Each worked example below demonstrates the exact level of detail and language required for full marks on Paper 5. Identify the command word in each sub-question; match the structure of the model answer to the strategy described in Section 5; and compare the mark scheme points explicitly identified in each answer. These are the standards you must meet, not examples you must memorise — the context of your Paper 5 exam will be different, but the experimental reasoning and language will be the same.
Worked Example 1: Planning — Rate of Reaction by Colorimetry

rows: 📝 WORKED ANSWER

QUESTION [6 marks]: A student wishes to investigate the effect of temperature on the rate of the reaction between hydrogen peroxide and iodide ions in acidic solution. The reaction produces iodine which forms a blue-black colour with starch. Describe a method the student could use to carry out this investigation.

IDENTIFYING VARIABLES [1]: 'The independent variable is temperature (range: 20°C to 60°C in 10°C increments). The dependent variable is the initial rate of reaction, measured using the time t for the blue-black colour to appear and calculating rate as proportional to 1/t.'
APPARATUS [1]: 'Equipment: water bath (±0.5°C) for each temperature; boiling tubes; stopwatch; 10 cm³ measuring cylinder or pipette; thermometer (±0.5°C) placed in the reaction mixture.'

PROCEDURE [1]: '(1) Set water bath to 20°C. (2) Measure 5 cm³ of 0.10 mol dm⁻³ H₂O₂ and 5 cm³ of 0.10 mol dm⁻³ KI (both acidified with dilute H₂SO₄) into separate boiling tubes. (3) Allow both solutions to equilibrate in the water bath for 5 minutes. (4) Confirm temperature using the thermometer.'

MIXING & TIMING [1]: '(5) Add a few drops of starch solution to the H₂O₂ tube. (6) Pour the KI solution into the H₂O₂ tube and simultaneously start the stopwatch. (7) Record the time t when the blue-black colour first appears. (8) Repeat twice at the same temperature; calculate the mean time.'

VARIATION & RANGE [1]: '(9) Repeat steps (1)–(8) at temperatures of 30°C, 40°C, 50°C and 60°C. Keep all other variables constant: [H₂O₂] = [KI] = 0.10 mol dm⁻³; total volume = 10 cm³; [H₂SO₄] = 0.05 mol dm⁻³.'

DATA PROCESSING [1]: '(10) Calculate rate proportional to 1/t for each temperature. (11) Calculate 1/T (in K⁻¹) for each temperature. (12) Plot ln(rate) on the y-axis against 1/T (K⁻¹) on the x-axis. A linear graph with negative gradient is expected (Arrhenius plot). The gradient = −Ea/R, where Ea is the activation energy and R = 8.31 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹.'

blocks.text: Worked Example 2: Graph Plotting and Gradient Calculation

rows: 📝 WORKED ANSWER
QUESTION [5 marks]: A student carried out a clock reaction at different concentrations of persulfate ions. The results are shown. Plot a graph of 1/t against [S₂O₈²⁻] and calculate the gradient.

AXES [1]: y-axis labelled '1/t / s⁻¹' (range 0 to at least 0.070); x-axis labelled '[S₂O₈²⁻] / mol dm⁻³' (range 0 to 0.60). Scale chosen so all points occupy more than half the grid in both directions.
PLOTTING [1]: All five points plotted correctly to within half a small square. Points marked with small crosses (×).

LINE OF BEST FIT [1]: A single straight line drawn through the data points, with approximately equal scatter above and below the line. The line passes close to (or through) the origin, consistent with a directly proportional relationship.

GRADIENT CALCULATION [2]: 'Using points read from the line of best fit: (0.10, 0.013) and (0.50, 0.067). These points are more than half the length of the drawn line apart. Gradient = (0.067 − 0.013) / (0.50 − 0.10) = 0.054 / 0.40 = 0.135 s⁻¹ / mol dm⁻³ = 0.135 dm³ mol⁻¹ s⁻¹. [1 for correct working shown; 1 for correct value and units]'

blocks.text: Worked Example 3: Evaluation — Error Analysis and Improvements

rows: 📝 WORKED ANSWER
QUESTION [4 marks]: Evaluate the reliability of the data collected using the clock reaction method described above. Suggest two improvements to the experimental procedure.

RANDOM ERROR — TIMING [1+1]: 'WEAKNESS: The endpoint of the clock reaction (appearance of blue-black colour) is subject to random error because the colour change is gradual. Different observers may record the time at different points in the colour development, introducing random error. IMPROVEMENT: Replace visual observation with a colorimeter set to measure absorbance at 580 nm (the peak absorbance of the iodine-starch complex). Define the endpoint as when the absorbance reaches a specific value (e.g., 0.50). This eliminates the subjective judgment of the endpoint.'

SYSTEMATIC ERROR — TEMPERATURE [1+1]: 'WEAKNESS: The temperature of the reaction mixture may not equal the temperature of the water bath, particularly at higher temperatures, because heat transfer is not instantaneous. This introduces a systematic error: the actual reaction temperature is lower than recorded. IMPROVEMENT: Place a thermometer directly in the reaction mixture and record the temperature at the moment of mixing. Allow at least 5 minutes equilibration time before combining the reagents, and repeat the temperature check immediately before mixing.'

blocks.text: Worked Example 4: Conclusion and Scientific Explanation

rows: 📝 WORKED ANSWER
QUESTION [3 marks]: Using your graph, draw a conclusion about the relationship between rate and [S₂O₈²⁻] and explain this in terms of collision theory.

WHAT THE DATA SHOWS [1]: 'The graph shows a linear (straight-line) relationship between 1/t (proportional to rate) and [S₂O₈²⁻], with the line passing through (or very close to) the origin. This indicates that the rate of reaction is directly proportional to the concentration of persulfate ions: rate ∝ [S₂O₈²⁻], consistent with first-order kinetics with respect to S₂O₈²⁻.'

COMPARISON TO PREDICTION [1]: 'This result supports the prediction that the reaction is first-order with respect to S₂O₈²⁻. The linearity of the graph and the small scatter of data points about the line of best fit provide reliable evidence for this relationship across the range tested (0.10–0.50 mol dm⁻³).'

SCIENTIFIC EXPLANATION [1]: 'This is explained by collision theory: as [S₂O₈²⁻] increases, the number of persulfate ions per unit volume increases proportionally. This increases the frequency of effective collisions between S₂O₈²⁻ ions and I⁻ ions per unit time, causing the rate of reaction to increase proportionally.'

blocks.text: Worked Example 5: Variables Identification

rows: 📝 WORKED ANSWER

QUESTION [4 marks]: A student is investigating how the concentration of sodium thiosulfate affects the rate of its reaction with hydrochloric acid. Identify the independent variable, dependent variable, and two control variables for this investigation.

INDEPENDENT VARIABLE [1]: 'The independent variable is the concentration of sodium thiosulfate, Na₂S₂O₃. This will be varied from 0.05 mol dm⁻³ to 0.40 mol dm⁻³ by diluting a stock solution of 0.40 mol dm⁻³ Na₂S₂O₃ with distilled water, keeping the total volume constant at 50 cm³.'

DEPENDENT VARIABLE [1]: 'The dependent variable is the rate of the reaction. This will be measured indirectly by recording the time t for a cross marked on paper beneath the flask to become invisible as the sulfur precipitate forms. Rate will be taken as proportional to 1/t.'

CONTROL VARIABLE 1 [1]: 'Temperature must be controlled because k (the rate constant) is temperature-dependent: even small temperature changes would change the rate independently of the concentration. Temperature will be controlled by carrying out all reactions in a water bath thermostatted at 25°C ± 0.5°C, with a thermometer in the reaction flask to confirm the temperature.'

CONTROL VARIABLE 2 [1]: 'The concentration and volume of hydrochloric acid must be controlled. A fixed volume of 10 cm³ of 2.0 mol dm⁻³ HCl will be used throughout, measured with a pipette (±0.06 cm³). If the acid concentration varied, the rate would change for reasons unrelated to the thiosulfate concentration.'

sections.title: SECTION 8: PREDICTED TOPICS FOR NEXT SESSION

blocks.text: Prediction Methodology for Paper 5

These predictions are based on: (1) 8-year analysis of Paper 5 experimental contexts and skill distribution; (2) identification of experimental contexts under-represented in 2023–2025; (3) Cambridge's increasing emphasis on quantitative data analysis; (4) examiner report commentary on persistent student weaknesses. Because Paper 5 tests skills (not topic-specific knowledge), the experimental context varies significantly year to year — but the SKILLS tested are entirely predictable.

rows: 🔑 KEY FACTS

The skills tested in Paper 5 are constant every year — planning, graphing, calculating, concluding and evaluating. It is the experimental CONTEXT that changes. Mastering the skills ensures marks regardless of context.

Rate of reaction experiments (using clock reactions, colorimetry, or gas volume measurement) are the single most common context in Paper 5. If this context has not appeared recently, it is highly likely to appear in the next session.
Thermometric experiments (measuring enthalpy change via temperature) appear regularly and are well-suited to Paper 5 evaluation questions — especially heat loss errors and calorimeter limitations.

Equilibrium/partition coefficient experiments and electrochemical cell experiments have appeared less frequently but are consistent with the A Level syllabus and could provide the context for future papers.
🔮 PREDICTION
Details
PREDICTION 1 — RATE OF REACTION EXPERIMENT (CERTAIN)

A planning question requiring design of an experiment to investigate the effect of a variable (concentration, temperature, or catalyst) on rate of reaction, using a clock reaction or colorimetry. Evaluation will focus on timing errors and temperature control. Practise writing complete method descriptions for rate experiments including: variables identification, control of temperature, data processing (1/t graph, Arrhenius plot).
PREDICTION 2 — ARRHENIUS PLOT / ln(rate) vs 1/T GRAPH (VERY LIKELY)

A data analysis question where rate data at different temperatures is provided, requiring: calculation of 1/T and ln(rate); plotting ln(rate) vs 1/T; calculating the gradient; using gradient = −Ea/R to find the activation energy Ea. Practise this specific graph type — it is the most common A Level graph in Paper 5.
PREDICTION 3 — THERMOMETRIC EXPERIMENT / ENTHALPY DETERMINATION (VERY LIKELY)

A planning or evaluation question based on measuring an enthalpy change by temperature measurement. Evaluation will focus on: heat loss error (systematic); imprecision of thermometer (random); incomplete reaction; assumptions in ΔH = mcΔT calculation. Practise writing paired weakness-improvement answers for each of these errors.
PREDICTION 4 — COLORIMETRY / ABSORBANCE vs CONCENTRATION (VERY LIKELY)

A data analysis question providing absorbance data at different concentrations, requiring: plotting absorbance vs concentration (Beer-Lambert calibration curve); using the graph to determine the concentration of an unknown solution; evaluating the precision of the colorimeter measurement.
PREDICTION 5 — PERCENTAGE ERROR CALCULATION (CERTAIN)

A calculation requiring the student to determine the percentage error in the most significant measurement in a given experimental procedure. This appears in virtually every Paper 5. Practise: identifying the measurement with the largest percentage error; calculating it using (2 × instrument uncertainty / measured value) × 100; comparing it to the overall precision of the result.
PREDICTION 6 — GRAPH WITH ANOMALOUS POINT (VERY LIKELY)

A graph plotting question where one data point is clearly anomalous. Mark scheme will award marks for: identifying the anomalous point by co-ordinates; drawing the line of best fit excluding that point; suggesting a reason for the anomaly; stating that the data point should be repeated.
PREDICTION 7 — PLANNING A TITRATION EXPERIMENT (LIKELY)

A planning question involving titration to determine a concentration or stoichiometry. Method must include: apparatus (burette, pipette, conical flask); concordancy (two titres within 0.10 cm³); rough titration; indicator choice; processing (calculation from mean titre). Evaluation will focus on burette precision and indicator endpoint sharpness.
PREDICTION 8 — PARTITION COEFFICIENT / SOLVENT EXTRACTION EXPERIMENT (MODERATE)

A planning question for a solvent extraction experiment to determine the partition coefficient of a solute between two immiscible solvents. Variables: volume/concentration ratio of solvents. Method must include: separating funnel; time for equilibration; analysis of each layer. This experimental context is underrepresented in recent papers.

blocks.text: How to Revise for Paper 5

Paper 5 revision requires a fundamentally different approach to revising for Papers 1, 2 and 4. Because it tests skills rather than knowledge, the primary revision method is PRACTICE — not reading or learning content. Every session must involve writing answers to past Paper 5 questions, comparing them against the mark scheme, and identifying the specific phrases and structural elements that were missing.

rows: 🔑 KEY FACTS

The most effective Paper 5 revision: write a complete planning answer from scratch, then compare sentence-by-sentence against the mark scheme. For every missed mark, identify the exact phrase or structural element required and add it to a 'key phrases' card.
Graph practice is essential — you must physically practice plotting points, choosing scales, and drawing lines of best fit on graph paper. This is a manual skill that cannot be revised by reading.

Do NOT neglect the laboratory component of your course. Paper 5 is specifically designed to reward candidates with genuine practical experience. The familiarity with equipment, error sources, and experimental logic that comes from real practical work cannot be fully replicated through written revision alone.

Evaluation answers improve most rapidly with a specific drill: for each experiment you study, write three paired weakness-improvement answers (one for each major error source). Compare against mark scheme. Repeat until your answers contain the exact level of specificity required.

blocks.text: Before You Enter the Exam Hall

Equipment: blue or black ink pen. HB pencil (for graph plotting, diagram drawing, mechanisms). Eraser. Ruler. Cambridge-approved scientific calculator. Protractor (occasionally useful for angle verification in graphs).
Arrive 15 minutes early. Mental warm-up: recall the 3 variable types, the 5-step gradient calculation procedure, and the paired weakness-improvement format. Do NOT read new content.

Write at the top of your answer booklet: 'IND = what I change AND range AND how. DEP = what I measure AND how. CONTROL = what I fix AND how.' This 30-second reminder prevents the most common planning error.

Also write: '% error = (2 × uncertainty / measurement) × 100' and 'Gradient: use 2 points > half line apart; show co-ordinates; units = y-units/x-units.' These prevent the most common data analysis errors.
In-Exam Discipline: The Non-Negotiable Rules

rows: Situation
The Correct Response
A planning question asks you to 'describe a method'

Write numbered steps. State specific quantities, concentrations, volumes. Describe how the independent variable is varied. Describe what graph to plot and how to extract the key value. Include safety. Never write a method without these elements.
A graph question requires plotting from a table with calculated values

Calculate all derived values first (1/t, ln rate, 1/T, etc.) before drawing the axes. Check the range of both axes from the data before choosing the scale. Never choose a scale without first knowing the full range.
You calculate a gradient but the units look wrong

Check: gradient units = y-axis units ÷ x-axis units. Write them out explicitly. If y is in s⁻¹ and x is in mol dm⁻³, gradient units are s⁻¹/(mol dm⁻³) = dm³ mol⁻¹ s⁻¹. Never leave gradient units blank.
An evaluation question asks for 'two improvements'

Write exactly two paired weakness-improvement answers. Each must: name the specific source of error → explain its effect on results → state a specific structural improvement. Do not write 'human error'. Do not write 'repeat the experiment' without specifying what structural change is made.
A conclusion question asks you to 'draw a conclusion from the graph'

Write 3 sentences: (1) describe the relationship shown (what shape, what direction, cite data values); (2) state whether it supports the hypothesis; (3) explain the relationship using chemistry. Never write only one of these three sentences.
You cannot determine the exact shape of the line of best fit (curve vs straight)

Draw what the data clearly indicates. If there is insufficient data to distinguish linear from curve, state this in writing: 'The graph appears linear across the tested range, but more data points at higher [X] values would be needed to confirm this relationship.'
You run out of time with one sub-part unanswered

Write at least the key structural element: for a planning question, state the independent and dependent variables; for an evaluation question, write one paired answer. Even 1 mark from a partial answer is better than zero.

blocks.text: The A* Paper 5 Mindset

rows: Typical Student Behaviour
A*-Standard Student Behaviour
Writes 'measure the volume and record the time' for a method
Writes 'measure 25.0 cm³ of 0.10 mol dm⁻³ Na₂S₂O₃ using a pipette; add 10.0 cm³ of 1.0 mol dm⁻³ HCl from a measuring cylinder; simultaneously start the stopwatch'
Identifies 'temperature' as a control variable without stating how to control it

States 'temperature will be controlled by thermostatting all solutions in a water bath at 25°C ± 0.5°C for at least 5 minutes before mixing, confirmed with a thermometer placed directly in the reaction vessel'
Calculates gradient using the first and last data points from the table
Identifies two points on the drawn line of best fit that are more than half the line apart, states their co-ordinates, shows the calculation, and gives units
Writes 'human error in timing' as an error source

Writes 'the colour change endpoint of the clock reaction is gradual; the time at which the cross becomes invisible is subject to random error because different observers may judge this moment differently; use a colorimeter to define a precise absorbance endpoint'
Writes 'the results support the prediction' as a conclusion

Writes 'the graph shows a directly proportional relationship (linear, through origin) between 1/t and [S₂O₈²⁻], supporting the prediction of first-order kinetics; this is consistent with collision theory as doubling [S₂O₈²⁻] doubles effective collision frequency'
✅ EXAMINER TIP

Paper 5 marks are won by specific, detailed, paired answers. Every vague phrase ('measure carefully', 'avoid errors', 'the results are reliable') that is not backed by a specific chemical or physical detail loses a mark. Replace every vague phrase with the specific apparatus, specific measurement, specific calculation, or specific structural improvement.

The graph is worth 4–5 marks in almost every Paper 5. These marks are among the most reliably available in the entire A Level — they require skill, not knowledge. A candidate who consistently loses graph marks for incorrect scale or missing units is leaving highly predictable marks on the table. Practice this skill until it is automatic.

Paper 5 is worth 11.5% of the A Level — equal to Paper 3. The skills it tests (planning and evaluation) are not naturally developed by reading textbooks or watching videos. They are developed by doing practical work and writing up methods and evaluations from experiments you have performed.

blocks.text: Essential Paper 5 Definitions — Mark Scheme Language

rows: Term
Full Mark Scheme Definition
Independent variable
The variable that is deliberately changed by the experimenter. The experimenter chooses the values. Must be stated with the range of values to be tested and how it will be varied.
Dependent variable
The variable that is measured as a result of changing the independent variable. Must be stated with the instrument and method of measurement.
Control variable
Any variable (other than the independent variable) that could affect the dependent variable, which must be kept constant throughout the experiment. Must be stated with the specific method of control.
Systematic error

An error that consistently biases the results in the same direction (all too high or all too low). Cannot be reduced by averaging repeated measurements. Examples: heat loss, zero error, light absorption by vessel.
Random error

An error that varies in both magnitude and direction between measurements. Reduced by repeating measurements and calculating a mean. Examples: timing a colour change, reading a meniscus, judging an endpoint.
Anomalous value (outlier)
A data value that lies significantly away from the trend shown by the other data points. Should be repeated; if confirmed anomalous, excluded from the line of best fit and from mean calculations.
Line of best fit

A single straight line or smooth curve drawn to show the general trend of the data, with approximately equal numbers of data points on each side along its entire length. Anomalous points must be excluded.
Concordant titres
Two successive titration values that differ by no more than 0.10 cm³. Only concordant values should be used in calculating the mean titre.
Prediction/hypothesis

A statement of the expected relationship between the independent and dependent variables, together with a chemical reason for the expected relationship. May be expressed as a written statement or a labelled graph sketch.
